content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Elizabeth Beryt 
Attorney 
Office of Chief Counsel 
PO Box 100 
Sacramento, CA 95812-0100 
November 10, 2015 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-15-196 
Dear Ms. Beryt: 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Board Member David Noren 
regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Nothing in this 
letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. In addition, this 
letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") 
does not act as the finder of fact. (111 re Oglesby ( 1975) l FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your questions 
are general in nature and not limited to specific governmental decisions, we are treating your 
request as one for informal assistance.2 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
QUESTION 
Under Regulation l 8703(e)(3), may Board Member Noren take part in governmental 
decisions regarding the Action Plan for the Russian River Watershed and related amendment to the 
Basin Plan despite owning a residential property within the watershed with an onsite wastewater 
treatment system that the decision may affect? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
' Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or fonnal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 


CONCLUSION 
File No. 1-15-196 
Page No. 2 
Provided that the low priority areas are based upon the total maximum daily load assessment 
as determined by water board staff, the public generally exception permits Board Member Noren to 
take part in decisions related to the Action Plan and related Basin Plan amendment barring any 
interest affected by the decision other than his residence. We caution, however, that the public 
generally exception only applies to the extent that there is evidence of a need to act within the low 
priority area. If the water board redefines those properties included within the low priority area, our 
conclusion may change depending on the evidence of the need to redefine and any unique effect on 
Counci lmember Noren' s residence. 
FACTS 
David Noren is a Board Member on the Regional Water Quality Control Board, North Coast 
Region (the "Regional Water Board"). The Regional Water Board is preparing an Action Plan for 
the Russian River Watershed Pathogen Indicator Bacteria Total Maximum Daily Load (the "Action 
Plan"). Board Member Noren owns real property with an onsite wastewater treatment system 
("OWTS") in the Russian River Watershed. 
Board Member Noren is one of six Regional Water Board members. The Regional Water 
Board regulates wastewater discharges to surface water and to groundwater. Water quality control 
plans provide the basis for protecting water quality in California. Both the Federal Clean Water Act 
and the State Porter-Cologne Water Quality Act mandate Basin Plans. The Basin Plan is the 
Regional Water Board's master water quality control planning document. It designates beneficial 
uses and water quality objectives for waters of the State, including surface waters and groundwater. 
It also includes programs of implementation to achieve water quality objectives. 
Section 303(d) of the Clean Water Act requires states to develop a list ofwaterbodies where 
required pollution control mechanisms are not sufficient or stringent enough to meet water quality 
standards applicable to such waters. The Russian River Watershed is listed as impaired for pathogen 
indicator bacteria. Because the Russian River Watershed is listed as impaired, the Regional Water 
Board is required to develop a total maximum daily load ("TMDL"), a calculation of the maximum 
amount of a pollutant that a waterbody can receive and still safely meet water quality standards, for 
the Russian River. If adopted, the Action Plan would amend the North Coast Regional Water 
Quality Control Board Basin Plan to include a program of implementation to achieve water quality 
objectives in the Russian River Watershed. 
The Action Plan describes the steps necessary to reduce pathogen concentrations and 
achieve the TMDL. The Action Plan identifies: ( 1) Actions that staff expects will reduce pathogens; 
(2) Parties responsible for taking these actions; (3) Regulatory mechanisms by which the Regional 
Water Board will ensure that these actions are taken; and (4) A timeline for completion of actions. 
As a component of the plan, the Action Plan would set requirements for owners of OWTS. These 
requirements would differ depending on whether the OWTS is located in a high, low, or non­
priority area in the Russian River Watershed. OWTS owners in a non-priority area would not need 
to comply with any additional requirements. Requirements for OWTS owners in low and high 
priority areas would lead to inspections, repairs, or new treatments technologies. 


File No. I-15-196 
Page No. 3 
The Action Plan would prohibit discharges of waste containing fecal waste material from 
humans or domestic animals to waters of the state within the Russian River Watershed that cause or 
contribute to an exceedance of the bacteria water quality objectives not authorized by waste 
discharge requirements or other order or action of the Regional or State Water Board. This 
prohibition would apply to a variety of discharges of waste. 
Board Member Noren is the owner ofreal property in the Russian River Watershed with an 
OWTS. The board member's property is located south of the City of Sebastopol within the Laguna 
de Santa Rosa watershed, a tributary of the Russian River. The property is located within 500 feet 
of an unnamed tributary to the Laguna de Santa Rosa. The property is 1.28 acres and is the board 
member's personal residence. You confirmed in a telephone conversation on November 5, 2015, 
that the property is not used for business purposes. As determined by the Regional Water Board 
Staff, Board Member Noren's residence is in a low priority area. As proposed in the draft Action 
Plan, OWTS within low priority areas may see additional inspection costs of approximately $1,000 
a year or every 5 years. 
The Russian River Watershed consists of approximately 1,484 square miles and serves as 
the water source for an estimated 500,000 residents within Mendocino, Sonoma, and Marin 
counties. The population of the municipalities within the watershed is approximately 286,038. An 
estimated 31-percent of the approximately 171,168 residences within the watershed (or 57,537 
residences) use OWTS. An Onsite Wastewater Treatment System Impact Study Report "con finned 
that OWTS contribute to the potential for pathogens.'' To address wastewater discharge, the Draft 
Action Plan and a Draft Staff Report both distinguish high priority areas, low priority areas, and 
nonpriority areas. In defining the priority areas, the Draft Staff Report states that high and low 
priority areas were "based on the [total maximum daily load] assessment by Regional Water Board 
staff." The Regional Water Board, however, "will further define and rank communities and other 
areas based on the threat to water quality from OWTS within these areas as new data become 
available." Currently, as identified in the Draft Basin Plan Amendment, low priority areas include: 
• Areas with a high density of OWTS in the middle and upper Russian River Watershed, 
including Oakmont in East Santa Rosa, North Cloverdale, Talmage, and Redwood Valley. 
• Areas where OWTS are within 600 linear feet in the horizontal (map) direction of the 
mainstem Russian River and the following tributaries of the Russian River in the middle and upper 
Russian River Watershed: Austin Creek, Big Sulphur Creek, Little Sulphur Creek, Commisky 
Creek, Dry Creek, Dutch Bill Creek, Feliz Creek, Fife Creek, Forsythe Creek, Franz Creek, Green 
Valley Creek, Laguna de Santa Rosa, Maacama Creek, Mark West Creek, Mill Creek, Pieta Creek, 
East Fork Russian River, Santa Rosa Creek, Sausal Creek, and York Creek. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
A conflict of interest may arise only when it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect on the official or his or her immediate family "distinguishable from its 
effect on the public generally." (Section 87103.) You have identified only Board Member Noren's 


File No. 1-15-196 
Page No. 4 
interest in his residence and asked whether the public generally exception applies notwithstanding a 
reasonably foreseeable material effect on the residence. 
The public generally exception applies only if the effect of a decision on the official's 
interest is indistinguishable for the effect on the public. Generally, an effect on the official is 
indistinguishable for the effect on the public if the official establishes that a significant segment of 
the public is affected and that there is no unique effect on the official. (Regulation 18703.} 
Regulation l 8703(b) provides the general rule for determining a significant segment, while 
subdivision (e) provides rules for special circumstances. In this case, the only provision of 
Regulation 18703 that appears to apply to the facts provided is Regulation 18703(e)(3). Under this 
rule, the financial effect on a public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from the effect 
on the public generally, if the official can establish: 
"Limited Neighborhood Effects. The decision affects residential real 
property limited to a specific location, and the decision establishes, amends, or 
eliminates ordinances that restrict on-street parking, impose traffic controls, deter 
vagrancy, reduce nuisance or improve public safety, provided the body making 
the decision gathers sufficient evidence to support the need for the action at the 
specific location." 
As adopted, this exception for decisions regarding public nuisances and public safety, 
permits officials to take part in decisions as a matter of public policy where there is sufficient 
evidence supporting the public purpose for the action, and the action widely applies to a specific 
location with no unique effect on the official. 3 
For decisions before the Regional Water District, decisions regarding wastewater discharges 
appear to fall within the scope of reducing a public nuisance and improving public safety. The 
pertinent questions are whether the decisions apply broadly to the public as a whole and whether 
there is sufficient evidence to support the need for the action at the specific location. 
In this case, the Russian River Watershed consists of approximately 1,484 square miles. It is 
estimated that 31-percent of the approximately 171,168 residences within the watershed (or 57,537 
residences) use an OWTS. In itself, the designation of the Russian River as an impaired waterway is 
significant evidence of a need to take action regarding the reduction of wastewater discharges 
within the watershed. Moreover, the Onsite Wastewater Treatment System Impact Study Report 
"confirmed that OWTS contribute to the potential for pathogens .... " Accordingly, to the extent 
that a decision will impose similar public safety standards on all OWTS within the watershed, it 
would be clear that the public generally exception in 18703(e)(3) applies. 
The Action Plan and Draft Staff Report, however, both distinguish high priority areas, low 
priority areas, and nonpriority areas. Because Board Member Noren's residence has been 
determined to be within a low priority area, we must further determine whether the decisions 
regarding low priority areas may have a unique effect on the board member and whether there is 
3 We also note that as used in the exception, the tenn "ordinance" has not been limited to formally adopted 
ordinances, but has been interpreted broadly to include actions of general applicability. (See Garibaldi Advice Letter, 
No. A-15-083.) 


File No. 1-15-196 
Page No. 5 
sufficient evidence of the need for action within the low priority area. In defining the priority areas, 
the Staff Report states that high and low priority areas were "based on the [total maximum daily 
load] assessment by Regional Water Board staff." The Regional Water Board, however, "will 
further define and rank communities and other areas based on the threat to water quality from 
OWTS within these areas as new data become available." As identified in the Draft Basin Plan 
Amendment, low priority areas include certain areas with a high density of OWTS and areas where 
OWTS are within 600 feet of the Russian River and tributaries. 
Based upon the facts provided, we can generally conclude that there appears to be sufficient 
evidence to support the action related to the low priority areas based upon the total maximum daily 
load assessment and that there does not appear to be any unique effect upon Board Member Noren's 
residence. To the extent that the low priority areas are based upon the total maximum daily load 
assessment as determined by Regional Water Board Staff, the public generally exception permits 
Board Member Noren to take part in decisions related to the Action Plan and Basin Plan 
amendment barring any other interest in the decisions other than his residence. 
We caution, however, that the public generally exception only applies to the extent that 
there is evidence of a need to act within the low priority area. Accordingly, if the Water Board 
redefines those properties included within the low priority area, our conclusion may change 
depending on the evidence of the need to redefine and any unique effect on Board Member Noren's 
residence. For example, if evidence does not support redefining an area in comparison to other areas 
or a specific decision removes Board Member Noren's residence from the low priority area without 
removing comparable properties, the public generally exception would not apply. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
BGL:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Brian G. Lau 
Counsel, Legal Division

written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

Counci lmember Noren' s residence.
